data.title: Swedish Krona Could Fall if Middle East Conflict Hits Growth — Market Talk
data.description: The Swedish krona looks set to weaken slightly as the Middle East conflict threatens to derail the country's economic recovery, Danske Bank's Stefan Mellin says in a note. Geopolitical uncertainty and the prospect of higher inflation stemming from rising energy prices pose "downside risks" to Danske Bank's expectation for a marked recovery and Swedish growth outperformance versus the eurozone in 2026, he says. The Riksbank and European Central Bank both look set to deliver two interest rate rises so the monetary policy outlook is neutral for the euro versus the krona, they say. Danske expects the euro to rise to 11.00 krona in six months, from 10.8093 at present. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

data.title: Regions Financial Posts Higher 1Q Profit, Revenue
data.description:
By Connor Hart

Regions Financial logged higher profit and revenue in the first quarter, reflecting what Chief Executive John Turner called continued momentum across markets despite an evolving macroeconomic backdrop.

The Regions Bank parents on Friday reported net income of $559 million, or 62 cents a share, compared with $490 million, or 51 cents a share, a year earlier.

Stripping out one-time items, earnings were also 62 cents a share. Analysts polled by FactSet expected adjusted earnings of 60 cents a share.

Revenue climbed 5% to $1.87 billion, just below Wall Street models for $1.91 billion.

Net interest income rose 4.5% to $1.25 billion, while non-interest income increased 5.9% to $625 million.

Provision for credit losses fell to $91 million from $124 million a year earlier.

Turner said growth in loans and deposits accelerated during the first quarter, while credit metrics continued to improve and client sentiment remained generally optimistic.

"At the same time, we are making meaningful progress on our core transformation, including key technology and AI investments that are enhancing efficiency and the customer experience, while remaining attentive to near‑term growth drivers," he added.

Write to Connor Hart at connor.hart@wsj.com

data.title: Cracks log weekly declines while hi-5 spread tightens
data.description:
Fuel oil cracks posted weekly declines in Asia, while the hi-5 spread narrowed sharply week-on-week.

Cracks for very low sulphur fuel oil (VLSFO) closed at a premium of about $10.25 a barrel on Friday, sliding nearly 30% for the previous week, LSEG data showed. (LFO05SGBRTCMc1)

Meanwhile, cracks for 380-cst high sulphur fuel oil (HSFO) closed at a premium of $2.45 a barrel, down about 5% from last week. (FO380BRTCKMc1)

Both markets have pared their strength in April so far, as prompt dates appeared to be amply supplied, trade sources said.

VLSFO weakened at a further extent than HSFO, which drove the hi-5 spread (FO05-380SGMc1) narrower by at least 30% week-on-week, closing near $50 a metric ton as of Friday.

INVENTORY DATA

- Fuel oil inventories in the ARA hub (STK-FO-ARA) rebounded in the week to April 16, climbing 5.6% to 702,000 tons, according to Dutch consultancy Insights Global.

OTHER NEWS

- Oil prices fell on Friday as possible further talks between the U.S. and Iran at the weekend and a 10-day ceasefire between Lebanon and Israel raised investor hopes that the war in the Middle East could be nearing an end.

- U.S. crude cargo prices have retreated from recent price spikes while prices in Europe and Asia continued to hit record highs almost seven weeks into the Iran war, as releases from the U.S. Strategic Petroleum Reserve and Venezuelan imports cushion domestic supplies, analysts and traders said.

- Indian oil refiners are doing their best to line up oil cargoes to meet local demand, an Indian government official said on Friday, after the United States decided not to extend a waiver from sanctions on crude purchases from Russia and Iran.

- A South Korean vessel carrying oil from Saudi Arabia exited the Red Sea on Friday in the first crude shipment to the country from the Middle East since the blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, the country's fisheries ministry said.

WINDOW TRADES

- 180-cst HSFO: No trade.

- 380-cst HSFO: Three trades.

- 0.5% VLSFO: Three trades.

data.title: Sterling Could Fall Vs Euro on BOE Rate Repricing — Market Talk
data.description: Sterling is likely to weaken against the euro as it looks vulnerable to markets pricing out Bank of England interest-rate rises, Danske Bank's Kirstine Kundby-Nielsen says in a note. If further cease-fire talks between the U.S. and Iran prove effective, the focus should return to fundamentals, she says. The U.K. economy remains fragile and there is scope for a more significant repricing of BOE rate expectations compared to the European Central Bank, she says. Danske expects the BOE to keep rates on hold throughout 2026 and 2027 while it sees the ECB raising rates in June and July. The bank expects the euro to rise to 0.89 pounds in six months from 0.8717 currently. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

data.title: AA, CENX Stocks Rally: Middle East Crisis Sends Aluminum Futures Near 4-Year Highs
data.description:
Key points:
  • AA and CENX shares have gained 20% and 28%, respectively, since the start of U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran on February 28.
  • Morgan Stanley recently upgraded Alcoa to ‘Overweight’ and raised the price target to $80, according to The Fly.
  • Apart from the conflict, domestic firms are also benefiting from Trump’s tariffs on aluminum imports.

Escalating tensions in the Middle East following U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran have roiled global aluminum markets, triggering disruptions and pushing futures to near four-year highs amid tightening supply.

Although the Middle East reportedly accounts for about 9% of the world's aluminum production, it plays a much larger role in global seaborne trade. LME aluminum prices have surged past $3,600 per tonne, with Goldman Sachs warning they could climb even higher if regional production losses persist, and Kpler analysts suggesting further escalation could push them toward $4,000 per tonne.

Against this backdrop, two companies have stood out - Alcoa (AA) and Century Aluminum (CENX) - with the shares climbing roughly 15% and 27%, respectively, since the war began.

While the stocks are tracking eleven consecutive months of gains, AA recently recorded its biggest weekly jump since December 2023.

On Thursday, Alcoa posted a 5.2% drop in first-quarter (Q1) revenue, falling below Wall Street’s estimates, according to Fiscal.ai data. However, earnings per share of $1.4 was in line with Street estimates, though they fell around 35% from last year.

Alcoa expects its 2026 aluminum production and shipments to remain unchanged, with output projected between 2.4–2.6 million metric tons and shipments between 2.6–2.8 million metric tons.

For Q2 2026, it anticipates a roughly $15 million sequential hit to earnings in the alumina segment due to weaker bauxite pricing and volumes, and higher energy costs driven mainly by diesel prices amid Middle East tensions.

A Perfect Storm for U.S. Producers

Beyond the broader conflict, CENX and AA have had an additional lift – President Donald Trump’s tariffs on aluminum imports. Trump raised aluminum tariffs to 50% in June 2025 in a bid to curtail cheaper foreign imports and increase domestic investment. Earlier this year, Century Aluminum and Emirates Global Aluminum (EGA) formed a joint venture to build a new smelter, a first such plant built in the U.S. in 50 years.

The supply shock deepened when Iranian missile and drone strikes hit EGA’s Al Taweelah smelter on March 28, with EGA warning restoration could take up to 12 months. Bahrain's Alba also declared force majeure after shutting down roughly 19% of its capacity due to disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz.

Wood Mackenzie estimates the conflict could remove 3 to 3.5 million tonnes of aluminum output in 2026. With the Gulf supply redirected, domestic producers stand to be the beneficiaries.

Analysts Remain Bullish, But Price Target Hinges On Conflict

According to The Fly, JPMorgan increased Alcoa’s price target to $70 from $68, but held a ‘Neutral’ rating, cautioning that the stock’s outperformance is tied directly to Middle East tensions and could reverse if geopolitical risks ease.

Morgan Stanley was more bullish, upgrading Alcoa to ‘Overweight’ from ‘Equal Weight’ and raising the price target to $80 from $64. The brokerage cited the upgrade to higher aluminum prices and Alcoa’s high operating leverage.

Wells Fargo lifted Century Aluminum’s price target to $77 from $69, and added that the outlook hinges on a projected 2026 supply deficit and elevated electricity costs. BMO Capital and B. Riley have also raised Century Aluminum’s target price.

How Did Retail Traders React?

Retail sentiment on Stocktwits for AA was ‘neutral’ and ‘bearish’ for CENX.

One user expects supply disruptions to persist for months, potentially pushing oil and aluminum prices to a higher floor over the long term.

https://stocktwits.com/Claytonpicker/message/649784937

Year-to-date, AA shares have gained around 35% while CENX stock has surged nearly 70%

data.title: Copper and aluminium trade in narrow ranges ahead of possible U.S.-Iran talks
data.description:
By Polina Devitt

Copper and aluminium were little changed on Friday as caution prevailed ahead of possible U.S.-Iran peace talks at the weekend.

Benchmark three-month copper on the London Metal Exchange (LME) was down 0.2% at $13,237.50 a metric ton by 1017 GMT. Having touched its highest since March 2 at $13,392.50 on Wednesday, the metal is up 3% this week.

Aluminium was down 0.2% at $3,635.50 a ton after hitting a four-year high of $3,672 in the previous session on expectations that the global aluminium market will face a supply deficit this year because of the Iran war.

U.S. President Donald Trump said the next meeting between the United States and Iran could take place over the weekend, adding that he believed the U.S. was "very close to making a deal with Iran".

A Pakistani security source told Reuters that Iran would open the Strait of Hormuz only if a permanent ceasefire is reached and there are United Nations guarantees that the U.S. and Israel will not attack again in the future.

Strong prices for copper, aluminium and other metals drove the LME's index of six base metals contracts (.LMEX) to a record high on Thursday on supply worries and investment from the wider financial system. Global equities were trading near record highs on Friday on the hopes for a peace deal.

Nickel rose 1.8% to $18,555 a ton after hitting $18,640 for its highest since January 29. Nickel is up 7.7% this week, poised for its strongest weekly growth in two years owing to expectations of reduced supply after Indonesia revised its ore price formula.

Among other LME metals, zinc added 0.2% to $3,428.50 a ton after hitting $3,446.5 for its highest since February 12. Lead gained 0.7% to $1,965 and tin was steady at $49,905.

data.title: Norwegian Krone's Recent Gains Unlikely to be Sustained — Market Talk
data.description: The Norwegian krone's recent strong performance is unlikely to be sustained, Danske Bank's Kristoffer Kjaer Lomholt says in a note. While the prospect of the Norges Bank raising interest rates is positive for the currency, Norway's negative output gap, higher inflation and wider unit labor cost gap compared to the eurozone make Norwegian assets less appealing, he says. "Also, we see the risk of a sharper downturn in the mainland economy which also skews the return distribution towards a sharper mover higher in euro-krone in the coming years." Danske expects the euro to rise to 11.50 krone in 12 months, from 11.0413 currently.(renae.dyer@wsj.com)

data.title: CAC 40 Up Nearly 0.5% On Iran Peace Hopes
data.description:
BRUSSELS (dpa-AFX) - The French stock market gained in strength after a steady start on Friday, as investors remain hopeful of U.S. and Iran engaging in meaningful discussions this weekend to bring an end to the ongoing conflict in the Middle East.

U.S. President Donald Trump has indicated that a second round of talks could take place this weekend, warning that fighting may resume if no deal is finalized.

The benchmark CAC 40 was up 33.55 points or 0.41% at 8,296.25 about a quarter past noon.

Dassault Systemes moved up 3.5%. Hermes International gained 3.4% and EssilorLuxottica climbed 3.1%. Capgemini advanced by about 2.7%.

Stellantis, STMicroelectronics, Saint Gobain, Renault, Bureau Veritas, Pernod Ricard, Eurofins Scientific, Sanofi, Airbus, Kering, Michelin, BNP Paribas and Publicis Groupe gained 1%-2%.

Orange drifted lower by about 3.4%. A consortium including the French telecom giant, Bouygues Telecom and Free-iliad Group has submitted an offer and started negotiations with the Altice France group to acquire SFR. Bouygues shares declined 1.8 percent.

Eiffage dropped by about 1.6%. TotalEnergies, Engie, Vinci, Veolia Environment and Carrefour lost 0.5%-0.9%.

In economic news, Eurozone exports decreased for the second straight month in February, data from Eurostat showed. Exports decreased 6.7% year-on-year in February, slower than the 7.7% fall in January. Likewise, imports dropped 2.2% from a year ago, following a 7.8% decrease in January.

As a result, the trade balance posted a surplus of EUR 11.5 billion, representing a substantial improvement from the EUR 1 billion deficit in January.

On a month-on-month basis, exports grew 0.9% in February and imports increased 3.5%. Consequently, the seasonally adjusted trade surplus fell to EUR 7.0 billion from EUR 12.8 billion in January.

data.title: U.K. Fiscal Concerns Could Resurface Ahead of May Local Elections — Market Talk
data.description: Concerns about the U.K.'s public finances and the government's ability to maintain fiscal prudence could resurface as the country heads into local elections in May, Bank of America strategists say in a note. The U.K. faces political risk as Prime Minister Keir Starmer faces pressure to step down after it emerged that former U.S. ambassador Peter Mandelson took up the role despite failing security vetting. Bond investors fear that a potential leadership change in the U.K. could open the door for looser fiscal rules and higher public borrowing. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

data.title: Hedge fund stock buying hits $86 billion as Iran peace hopes, Goldman data shows
data.description:
By Nell Mackenzie

Systematic hedge funds boughts stock at a record pace in the last five trading sessions, adding $86 billion of stock exposure, said a Goldman Sachs note published late Thursday and seen by Reuters on Friday shows.

Such hedge funds use algorithms to ride market trends until they peter out and use market signals rather than views on the economic aspects of the company stocks they trade.

World stocks held near record highs on Friday, set for their a third straight week of gains, as investors awaited a crucial weekend that could pave the way for a near-term resolution to the Middle East war.

Key points from the Goldman Sachs data:

  • The last five sessions have garnered one of "the largest in history" trading demand from CTAs (Commodity Trading Advisors), or hedge funds that ride market trends.

  • Since markets turned around at start of April, hedge funds — particularly those that trade systematically — have been equity buyers, betting that asset values would rise.

  • The rate at which speculators have bought global equities over the last week is in the top five buying speeds of all time.

  • Goldman Sachs calculations estimate that these speculators could continue to buy stocks and purchase an additional $70 billion of the next five sessions.

  • The last time global CTAs bought stocks with this fervor was in August 2024, November 2023 and September 2019.

data.title: Truist reports 1Q26 net income $1.48B; EPS $1.09, revenue $5.15B
data.description:
Truist reported first-quarter 2026 net income of $1.48 billion and net income available to common shareholders of $1.38 billion, producing diluted EPS of $1.09 (up 25% year-over-year). Total revenue was $5.15 billion (taxable-equivalent $5.20 billion) and taxable-equivalent net interest income was $3.64 billion; the company returned significant capital to shareholders via $1.1 billion of share repurchases and common dividends. Truist maintained strong asset quality and capital ratios, with a preliminary CET1 ratio of 10.8% and an ALLL ratio of 1.53% at quarter end.

Financial Highlights

  • Net income: $1.48 billion for 1Q26; net income available to common shareholders: $1.38 billion.
  • Diluted EPS: $1.09 for 1Q26, up 25% versus 1Q25.
  • Total revenue: $5.15 billion (TE: $5.20 billion); taxable-equivalent net interest income: $3.64 billion (TE).
  • Noninterest income: $1.55 billion; total noninterest expense: $2.98 billion, yielding an efficiency ratio of 57.9%.
  • Provision for credit losses: $479 million; net charge-offs annualized: 0.61% of average loans and leases.

Business Highlights

  • Loan growth driven by commercial portfolio: average loans and leases HFI rose to $327.0 billion (up 0.7% linked quarter), led by commercial and industrial and CRE increases.
  • Deposit trends: average deposits increased to $398.9 billion (up 0.7% linked quarter); end-of-period deposits were $404.1 billion.
  • Revenue mix and client activity: investment banking and trading income increased, wealth management income rose on higher AUM, and mortgage banking income improved on higher production.
  • Capital return and balance sheet actions: repurchased $1.1 billion of common stock and declared common dividends of $0.52 per share; dividend and total payout ratios were 47% and 129%, respectively.
  • Asset quality and reserves: nonperforming assets were $1.8 billion; ALLL remained stable at 1.53% of loans and leases HFI with an ACL of $5.3 billion (including reserves for unfunded commitments).

Original SEC Filing: TRUIST FINANCIAL CORP [ TFC ] - 8-K - Apr. 17, 2026

Disclaimer
This is an AI-powered summary. It may contain inaccuracies. Consider verifying important information with the source. Please note this summary is solely based on documents filed with the SEC.
